Before the runners had even cooled down, speculation arose as to what had gone wrong with Uta. Some said it was the humidity, others overconfidence, overtraining, or her shoes. Whatever the reason, Uta wasn't saying. She disappeared after the race and reporters could not get a comment from her. "We never saw Uta," says journalist Woelk. "She never appeared at the stadium, and even the German officials didn't have an explanation about what happened. She was nowhere to be found."

Says Benji Durden, "With Uta it's always hard to tell how good of shape she is in. Leading up to a marathon, she doesn't run a lot of races. Obviously, she was very fit for Atlanta, so it [dropping out] had to be something else."

A few days after the marathon, Pippig told the German newspaper Bilt that she had been bothered early in the race by a pinched sciatic nerve in her back. She said she "could not move well," and by 15 kilometers knew "this was not going to be it." Uta said she will continue training through the Sydney 2000 Olympics, and she still holds on to her dreams of getting both an Olympic gold medal and Kristiansen's marathon world record—two dreams she's held for years.

Despite the Olympic DNF, Uta won respect for her front-running tactic. Says Arturo Barrios, "Oh yes, I think she did the right thing. She was going for the win. Basically, when you run like Uta did, you are going to scare people. She did, and they let her go. They knew she was the best one. At one point she had the big lead, but when the pack realized the gap was remaining the same, they came back. But I think it was a very good strategy. You have to attack; she had a bad day, but that is a chance you have to take. She went for it."

That's what champions do.

Adapted and updated by permission from Running with the Legends by Michael Sandrock (Human Kinetics, 1996).

figures.caption: Uta Pippig sets her sights on becoming the first woman to break 2:20 in the marathon.
With the guidance and love of coach and companion Dieter Hogen, Uta has blossomed into the most formidable female distance star in the 90s.
As early as 1987, Uta and Dieter dreamed of escaping from East Germany.
Uta divides her time between Berlin and Boulder, where she runs most of her workouts on hilly courses.
Uta plans to be a medical doctor after her competitive running days are over.
Uta's 1996 Boston Marathon victory was one of the most heroic in the race's 100-year history.

articles.title: My Most Unforgettable Marathon . . .
articles.body_html:
YONKERS, NEW YORK, May 13, 1962—Most marathoners associate me with the Boston Marathon. I must admit that after starting 61 Bostons and finishing 58 of them, I fondly make that association myself. I also won Boston twice, in 1935 (2:32:07) and in 1945 (2:30:40). I also have the dubious distinction of being a "bridesmaid" of record proportions—I took second place seven times. I also finished in the top 10 at Boston some 18 times.

From the hindsight of 88 years on this earth, I look back at my life as a runner and can't help but feel blessed. I've met so many wonderful people through running and have so many fond memories. Of course, like all runners, especially marathoners, I have also experienced my share of crushing defeats. It seems to me that sometimes we learn more by losing than we do by winning—which is fortunate, since only one person wins a race while the rest of us learn our lessons for that day and apply them to the next race.

When the subject of the single most unforgettable marathon in my long running career comes up, I find I must shift gears (for the uphills) and shift states (to New York). My most unforgettable and most satisfying marathon is my 2:37:42 fourth-place finish at the May 13, 1962, Yonkers National Marathon. What makes that race so special to me is the fact that I was 54 years old when I ran it.

In the showers afterwards, Ted Corbitt, one of the great pioneers of our sport, said to me, "You are running better now than when you were champion." Coming from Ted, that was a mighty compliment and one that I hold dear to this day.

YONKERS NATIONAL MARATHON

Before I tell you about my 1962 Yonkers race, let me tell you about the Yonkers National Marathon, since few people have ever run the race, even though it's been around in one form or another since Thanksgiving Day 1907.

How can I best summarize Yonkers? It was a darn tough course and became even more so after the date for the race was moved from Thanksgiving to a month after Boston, which placed it in a time of year when the city is traditionally hot and humid. And, like Boston, the race has traditionally had a noon start. The course was also hilly, most of the surface was unforgiving concrete (a marathoner's worst enemy), there was often a shortage of water on the course, and there was traffic all over the course. Yonkers was run on a course "real" marathoners in those days respected—and feared.

I won Yonkers the first time in 1935, the same year I won my first Boston. But in 1935, Boston was in April and Yonkers on Thanksgiving Day. My win in the 1935 race marked the revival of Yonkers, which was first run in 1907. The 1912 race was skipped, and then the race died on the vine after the 1917 running. When it was revived in 1935, it also became the AAU (Amateur Athletic Union, a forerunner to today's USATF) championship marathon, a distinction it held until 1966. For a good part of that period, the United States picked its Olympic marathon team by requiring runners to enter Boston in April and Yonkers in May. The AAU then averaged the finishing times of each runner to pick the team that would represent the United States at the Olympics. Running two hard and hilly marathons within five weeks might explain why we didn't do very well in the Olympic marathon until that system was changed.

During its heyday, the Yonkers National Marathon was run by the Chippewa Democratic Club, which was responsible for raising the race from the ashes in 1935. The Chippewa Club was really something. I guess by today's standards the club would be considered very politically incorrect—the members were old white men who had fat bellies and smoked cigars, but without them, the race would never have existed.

[[FIGURE:2]]

When Yonkers was revived in 1935, the start and finish was on the old Empire City Race Track, which was sandy feeling and hard to run on. We started the race with one mile on the track and ended with four miles on the track. It was horrible. In 1949, I think, the track was being rebuilt or something, and the course was moved away from the track. The race began and finished near the Chippewa Club house, and the date was changed from November to May. In 1950, at age 42, I won the race again, in 2:45:55, on the new course. Along with the win came the AAU Championship. Winning Yonkers 15 years apart remains one of my proudest achievements.

Besides the fact that it is usually hot and humid in May in Yonkers, New York, the Yonkers course is similar to Boston because it saves its worst hills for late in the race when the runners are all worn out. The worst hills begin after 18 miles, out along the Hudson River, and they have been the "graveyard" for many a fine runner.

MY TRAINING

You must remember that most of us who were running competitively in those days held full-time jobs. Clarence DeMar, who won Boston seven times, worked as a printer. John J. Kelley of Groton, Connecticut (no relation to me but a great friend), worked as a schoolteacher. Young John came into the 1962 Yonkers with six straight wins—on his way to eight in a row.

I had what I consider to be a very good work schedule at Boston Edison. I worked every other Sunday as a regularly scheduled day, for which I received a premium in my wages. During these weeks, I had one day off—Saturday. Every other week I had three days off—Friday, Saturday, and Sunday.

I worked 40 hours a week and could have had all the overtime I wanted, but if I had taken that extra work, I wouldn't be here now. I did a lot of work with asbestos—how I failed to get sick, I'll never know. The men who worked with me also smoked cigarettes, and they've been dead for more than 15 years.

During the weeks with the long weekends, I came home on Thursday night and had a big dinner. I would not run that night. But on Friday, my first day off, around 8:00 or 8:30 A.M., I'd do my long, long run of two to two and a-half hours. I never ran on mileage—only on the watch.

After my long run on Friday, I'd do all my chores and whatever. Saturday morning I might run 45 minutes, and that's about it. Then Sunday, I'd run an hour and 20 minutes, nice and easy. I packed all my mileage in on Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. Monday to Thursday were rest days.

The following week when I had only one day off, Saturday, I'd just run an hour or an hour and 20 minutes or so. I didn't run on any of the other days during the weeks when I had only Saturday off.

This training schedule was the secret of my success as far as the Yonkers Marathon goes. I sincerely believe that not running too much during the week brought me into the race rested.

THE 1962 YONKERS RACE

I had run Boston the month before the 1962 Yonkers race, finishing in 25th place with a 2:44:36. I had run almost the same time the year before and placed 17th. The 1962 Boston Marathon had quite a race up front. Finland's Eino Oksanen won for the second year in a row. He'd had a real duel with Young John Kelley the year before. Another Finn, Paavo Pystynen, took second, and Alex Breckenridge, a U.S. Marine lieutenant, took third, with Young John in fourth.

But back to Yonkers. It rained the day before, but raceday was as nice a day for a race as I can remember. The temperature at the start was in the high 50s. About 150 runners signed up, but only 71 starters lined up. Two years before in this race I had placed 12th, and the previous year I was 10th.

I remember that Harvey Listenstein, who'd won the 1909 Yonkers race, was at the start. He was 71 years old at that time.

I started out conservatively, but pretty soon I began passing people. I was running comfortably with Ed Duncan of the Boston Athletic Association and Jack Rubin. (The BAA would go on to win the team competition that year, taking first, second, and sixth.)

The following is the journal entry made by John J. (Young John) Kelley, whose 1962 win at Yonkers was his seventh out of eight in a row—Ed.

"Noon: Yonkers National Marathon—Won in 2:27:39, over Alex Breckenridge (who finished) 2nd in 2:30:46. Ran hard with Alex over first 10 (miles), pulling away at 11+, then building up substantial lead only to experience deadening in last 5 miles, so that (my) time at 20 (miles), 1:48:58, indicating possible 2:20, proved only (the) slowness of the final 6 (miles). Took oranges and water dousings frequently after 22 miles. George (Terry) (was) 3rd in 2:32, Old John (A. Kelley) 4th, 2:37. Weather fair and mild (75 degrees, south-southeast breeze). Miles run to date this year, 1,467."

Young John adds: "(Old) John's performance certainly did—and does—stand out, notwithstanding every competitor's regard for his seemingly ageless formidability. A curious thing about that regard for him was that, while we 'young Turks' never expected him to beat us, we could always come up a bit shocked by how much in the thick of it he kept proving himself. This Yonkers race was an excellent case in point—maybe the best such. I can still remember at the time thinking back to 1950 when he had won the National Championship at Yonkers at age 42!"

Together we went through 10 miles out near Hartsdale in around seventh place with a 57:44. Young John Kelley had gone through a little more than three minutes before us. At 15 miles at Elmsford, I was five minutes behind Young John, but I had moved up to sixth. I kept my pace steady, and little by little I caught two more runners and found myself in fourth place. I couldn't believe it, yet I could believe it because even at age 54, I felt competitive, and I felt rested—and I knew the course. I remained in fourth, finishing in 2:37:42.

Young John, as he usually did, ran a marvelous race. He was in control the whole way. He ran a 2:27:39, in the process beating BAA teammate Alex Breckenridge (2:30:46), who'd beaten him at Boston. George Terry, Young John's brother-in-law, took third in 2:32:21.

The training I'd been using all along—with lots of training on three days, then no training but regular work for four days—had worked for me for years, and I guess it really came together on that day in Yonkers.

Even as I approach 90 years of age, I can still vividly remember the 1962 Yonkers race. I love the Boston Marathon, but those of us who used to race marathons regularly back in those days also have a really special place in our hearts for Yonkers. When you raced well there, you knew it—and so did everyone else.

[[FIGURE:1]]

What I Learned . . .

AGING AND DEATH AREN'T THE SAME THING

Just because a runner adds a few years doesn't mean that he or she is over the hill. There are a lot of good older runners because when you get older, if you're still out there running and racing, you've learned a few things about how to pace yourself. Runners don't lose that much performance for each year they age. They just have to take better care of themselves, and they need to. . .

REST MORE

I've seen my share of very talented runners literally run themselves into the ground by training too much, resting too little, and racing way too often. We need to rest our bodies so they can recover from hard workouts. My job at Boston Edison didn't give me a lot of options. Oh, I could have gone out in the dark after work and run a few miles during the week, but I was already tired from putting in a long, hard day of work. What my body craved was some nourishing food and some rest so that when the weekend came, I could run strong again. If you overtrain, you can leave your best races on your training courses. Resting at the right time gives you longevity. Trust me on this.—J.K.

figures.caption: Most marathoners associate Johnny Kelley with the Boston Marathon, which he won twice, here in 1945.
Kelley finishes the 1946 Yonkers National Marathon with four miles on the dreaded Empire City Race Track.
Following a life-long training program that stressed quality and rest, Johnny Kelley continues to run, even as he approaches his 90th birthday. COURTESY OF JOHN A. KELLEY

articles.title: The Art of Peaking
articles.body_html:
No running distance is easier to prepare for than the marathon. The primary variable you deal with when training to run or race 26.2 miles is lactate threshold. Unlike shorter events like the 5K and 10K, in which you must train your body to run successfully after lactate forms in the bloodstream, you can successfully run a marathon only when you have trained your body to operate at lactate threshold (one's anaerobic threshold).

You can't successfully complete a marathon once large amounts of lactate have accumulated in the bloodstream. All those reactions you studied in biology (Krebs cycle, glycolysis and respiration, ATP, ADP, etc.) become your worst nightmare once your pace exceeds the lactate threshold. The key to marathoning, then, is to train your body to increase the lactate threshold pace while avoiding the formation of lactic acid in the bloodstream. Marathon training is that simple. Trust me.

But if it's so simple, why is the marathon the most feared yet revered distance in the running world? Because, if done unwisely, the training required to reach the potential lactate threshold pace could open a Pandora's Box of injury, illness, fatigue, and the powerful temptation to open a bottle of your favorite beverage and sit on the couch rather than lace up your running shoes and head out for a run.

Let me offer a conceptual framework that will help you understand the discussion on marathon training that follows. At times throughout the article, I will refer to beginners (new to running, whose goal is just to finish the marathon), advanced (whose goal is to improve on a finishing time or reach a certain time goal), and elites (whose goal is to run a national- or world-class time). Examine Figure 1.

[[FIGURE:1]]

Numbers 1, 2, and 3 on the left side—aerobic capacity training, lactate threshold training, and V̇O₂max training—represent the independent variables that you must achieve while training for your marathon. If you're successful, then you will achieve the dependent variables on the right side. Threshold training is the key variable. But, without aerobic capacity training and V̇O₂max training, you cannot do your best threshold training. Let's look at each one.

Aerobic Capacity Training

Aerobic capacity training or running economy training (RE) allows you to advance your aerobic fitness level so you are training comfortably and consistently while stressing quantity over quality. You do this training by consuming submaximal amounts of oxygen as you run; that is, you're not struggling for breath, and your muscles aren't starving for oxygen as you run.

Your aerobic capacity advances by receiving a constant dose of consistent mileage done over several weeks' time. What kind of training levels are involved? Up to 25 miles for beginners, 50 miles for advanced runners, and 80 miles for elites. Weekly mileage increases by approximately 10 percent every three weeks. Beginners run 15 miles per week for three weeks, then 17, then 20, then 25 three months later. Advanced runners log 35, 40, 45, then 50 miles. Elites run 70, 80, 90 miles, or more, depending on background. After a two- to three-month regimen of RE training, it's time to incorporate the two other variables necessary to run a successful marathon.

Lactate Threshold Training

Threshold training refers to anaerobic threshold and lactate threshold. For the sake of this discussion, suffice it to say that they mean the same thing. In a nutshell, if you run continuously at a pace that is too fast for your body to comfortably utilize oxygen, lactate forms in the bloodstream. If you reach this pace too early in a race, your body builds up lactate until it is impossible for you continue at that pace—or at all, for that matter. Obviously, the faster you can run without triggering lactate production, the faster you'll get to the finish line.

Your lactate threshold (LT) pace is 10 to 15 seconds per mile slower than your 10K race pace (which is approximately your 15K or 10-mile race pace). LT workouts are most satisfying because you can do them week after week with a noticeable increase in your fitness level over time. And these LT workouts are not so demanding on your body that it rebels against the thought of doing them. In contrast, training toward a faster 5K or 10K requires quick and demanding workouts.

Sample LT Workouts

The simplest LT workout is the standard tempo run, a 20- to 25-minute run done at 10 to 15 seconds slower per mile than your current 10K race pace. With a sufficient RE base under your belt, four months out from your marathon, you'd run one day a week at LT pace (in the case of the 45-minute 10K runner, that would be one 3-mile run at 7:30 pace). As your fitness improves during these four months, increase up to 15 percentage the amount of weekly mileage run at LT pace. So, by two months out from the marathon, a 40-mile-per-week, 45-minute 10K runner would log 6 miles (in two 3-mile runs) per week at 7:30 pace. The 30-minute 10K elite runner doing 100 miles per week should build up to handling 15 miles per week at LT pace (5:05 pace), divided into two or even three days.

As Table 1 shows, other types of LT workouts include long repetitions, such as 4 to 5 × 1 mile at 10K or 15K race pace with a three- to five-minute recovery between each, or 6 to 8 × 1,000 meters at 10K or 15K race pace with a two- to four-minute recovery between each. These workouts can be done on the track, trail, grass, road, or other surface.

Any successful training program always carries one warning: allow flexibility and room for adjustment if necessary. If you're tired, skip the tempo run. If you're bored with the route you've been using, do the tempo run elsewhere, hook up with someone else, use a race as your tempo run, or do another type of LT workout.

TABLE 1   Sample Lactate Threshold Workouts
Name Pace Time When to do them Frequency
*Tempo run 10–15 seconds per mile slower than 10K pace 25 min. Four months out / Two months out Once a week / Twice a week
*Long reps 10K or 15K pace 3+ min. Four months out / Two months out Once a week / Twice a week

*Run a tempo run and long reps on alternate weeks.

V̇O₂max Training

V̇O₂max workouts are short, intense intervals at 5K race pace. This type of pure "speedwork" is very grueling both mentally and physically, and should only be undertaken by those fit enough to survive it. Examples of V̇O₂max work include about two miles worth of intervals lasting anywhere from a minute and a half to three minutes in length, with an equal recovery in between. These workouts train your body to better utilize oxygen by training it to consume oxygen while you are running very fast.

Mileage Guidelines

Table 2 shows marathon finishing time goals and the least weekly mileage necessary to obtain the time (all are ballpark figures). At least four months out from your marathon, you should be logging at least 40 miles a week if you wish to actually race it for time. If merely finishing is your goal, build up to 30 miles or so just before the marathon. But for you racers (advanced and elites), follow these guidelines:

  • Five months from your marathon, run at least 70 percent of your ultimate goal for maximum miles per week.
  • Four months out from your marathon, run at least 80 percent of your ultimate goal for maximum miles per week.
  • Two months out from your marathon, run your maximum weekly mileage until two weeks prior to marathon, when you will begin to taper.
TABLE 2   Weekly Mileage Totals Based on Time Goal
Time goal Five months out Four months out Two months out
2:15–2:22 60 68 85
2:22–2:35 53 60 75
2:35–2:50 42 48 60
2:50–3:30 35 40 50
3:30–4:00 32 36 45
4:00+ 21 24 30

The numbers in Table 2 aren't etched in stone. I provide them as a guideline to prevent overtraining in the preparation phase of marathon training. The higher the mileage, the more likely advanced and elite runners will be able to reach their goal, as long as they don't overtrain. For beginners, running more slow, easy miles doesn't necessarily mean they will run faster. In the early months of marathon training, the mileage should be comfortable to handle, so that lactate threshold and VO2max training can be thrown into the training mix as early as possible. If you can't handle the mileage, then you need to advance your aerobic capacity further before you can do more arduous training.

How much should first-time marathoners run, and how fast? For novice marathoners, more is not necessarily better. If you're running 35 miles per week with four weekly workouts, moving up to six similar-intensity workouts and 50 miles per week probably won't boost your marathon performance. Your performance times will improve after you've incorporated the fast-paced training into the weekly mileage mix geared specifically for the marathon.

Training at faster than marathon velocity will produce a higher fitness level compared to slower running, but it doesn't teach the body to function well over a marathon-type span of time. Training long and slowly accomplishes the latter, but produces smaller gains in fitness. If your goal is simply to finish, then don't worry about a fast pace. Build up to 30 to 45 miles per week, occasionally running long (up to 150 minutes) so you have confidence that you can complete the distance.

BUILDING A BASIC TRAINING PLAN

My basic training plan for marathoners of all abilities prescribes mileage totals and a time frame to run these miles, based on a goal finishing time. If your goal is merely to finish a marathon, you only need to run easy miles leading up to the big day, perhaps throwing in a few 20-milers. But if you want to reach an actual time goal, you've got to do more specific training.

What About Pace and Timing?

What should these weekly miles consist of in terms of pace? What are the workouts trying to accomplish? And when should you run a specific workout? First, you should run easy RE mileage five or six days a week up to five months out from your marathon. This allows your body to increase its aerobic capacity, thereby laying the foundation for more arduous workouts, which should begin weekly about four months out from the marathon. If you have trouble adjusting to the faster-paced workouts, then continue to build on your aerobic capacity. Difficulty with faster running is your body's way of saying it's not quite ready.

Incorporating faster running (LT training) four months out from your marathon is a ballpark estimate, so don't sweat it if you're not quite ready for the fast stuff. Table 3 shows you what your weekly mileage paces should be and what these paces accomplish.

TABLE 3   Weekly Mileage Mix
Type of workout Pace Quantity Purpose
Running economy 1:00–2:00 per mile slower than LT pace 80% Recovery, aerobic and musculoskeletal strength
Lactate threshold 10–15 seconds per mile slower than 10K race pace (or 15K pace) Up to 15% Dealing with marathon pace
VO2max 5K race pace 5% biweekly Increase oxygen consumption
  • Run 80 percent of your weekly miles at running economy pace, which is your most relaxed pace, or the pace you can run while carrying on a conversation. In general, this pace is about one to two minutes slower per mile than your LT pace. RE pace is designed to help you recover from the fast stuff while building your aerobic and musculoskeletal strength. It is also designed to help you relax and remember why you enjoy running in the first place. Let's face it, the fast stuff ain't easy.
  • Run up to 15 percent of your weekly miles at LT pace. LT pace helps your body prepare for the specifics of maintaining marathon pace. You want your body to get used to running right at the border where lactate forms. LT pace is the fastest pace possible to train at without becoming unable to finish the workout. The 15 percent figure is again ballpark. Don't fret if you only run 5 percent of your weekly miles at LT pace. You will still be building up your body to prepare for marathon pace. If you can handle the 15 percent total, you know you are ready to meet your ultimate time goal.
  • If you can handle 15 percent of your weekly miles at LT pace, then every other week run 5 percent of your weekly miles at VO2max pace. Otherwise, run the remaining weekly miles at RE pace. As a matter of fact, begin the every-other-week regimen only during the final six to eight weeks of your marathon preparation, and only if you are aiming for a sub-3:15 marathon. Again, be careful with this stuff. It's like playing with fire!

What Do the Numbers Mean?

Let's apply the percentages in Figure 4 to a 40-mile-per-week individual who wants to run a 3:10 marathon. This runner can log a 5K in 21:30. Double this time and add a minute to get an idea of 10K pace. This person would be in roughly 44-minute 10K shape. Therefore, this runner would run 32 miles at 8:20 per mile (RE pace), 5 miles at 7:20 per mile (LT pace), and two miles at 6:55 per mile (VO2max pace). Hence, we've established a training regimen to begin about four months prior to the marathon.

FINE TUNING

After your last long run, three weeks before the marathon, resist the urge to do another one. You will have done sufficient work up to three weeks out. You can't get any fitter. The best thing for your body is rest. However, this doesn't mean you should stop running altogether.

You may want to jump in a local road race two weeks before your marathon to get a feel for the racing atmosphere. The weekend before the marathon, go out for an easy 10-miler. Practice your form, feel your relaxed strides. Imagine yourself running the marathon easily, effortlessly.

Bolster your confidence by reviewing your recent months of training. Remember how you went from running 10 miles at first to your last 20-miler, which felt more comfortable than the 10-miler. Be confident that the money is in the bank, and you're ready to cash the check on raceday.

No later than two weeks before the marathon (and even four weeks if you've been training at a high intensity), you must cut back on your training. Two weeks before, cut back 20 to 40 percent of your previous weekly mileage. One week before cut back 40 to 60 percent. Run one workout two weeks before of three to four miles at LT pace, unless you've done a road race.

During the last week before the race, do some 400-meter repeats after warming up with one mile. The 400s should be quick and relaxed. Run up to 6 × 400 meters five days before the race, then 3 × 400 meters four days before. If you've been training at a high intensity, run another 3 × 400 meters three days before. See Table 4 for a sample last week of training.

Running 400s works the leg muscles enough to allow them to replenish great amounts of necessary fuel (glycogen) in the muscles without taxing them too much. This is the week to eat a lot of pasta and other high carbohydrate foods. Also, don't do any long runs during the final two weeks: one longer run of 10 to 12 miles the weekend before the marathon is enough.

TABLE 4   Marathon Week Training
Day Workout Purpose
1 Easy 1-hour run Gain confidence, feel relaxed
2 Easy 30- to 40-minute run Gain confidence, feel relaxed
3 Easy 1 mile, 6 × 400m quick and relaxed Lightly taxing leg muscles, allowing subsequent glycogen storage
4 Same as Day 3, except run 3 × 400m Lightly taxing leg muscles, allowing subsequent glycogen storage
5 Same as Day 4 Lightly taxing leg muscles, allowing subsequent glycogen storage
6 Easy 10-minute run Rest
7 Marathon

MENTAL PREPARATION

At this point, one week from the marathon, there isn't much more you can do to prepare physically for the race, but training goes beyond the physical. You can add some final touches before the gun goes off to be at your mental peak come raceday.

Try to get a couple of days of complete rest before the race. The day before the race, you may want to stretch your legs, probably by walking or by doing an easy jog for about 10 minutes. Don't overexert or overexcite yourself. This is not the day to walk around the expo all afternoon, nor is it the time to concern yourself with finances or other life issues—you know, the things that can "play with your mind" if you let them. Instead, make sure you've taken care of all the little things, the details that will help you stay relaxed and minimize problems on raceday. To help you, here's a prerace checklist:

  • Get a good night's sleep two nights before the race. Research has shown this is when sleep will benefit you the most. The night before your race, set up an alarm clock or get a wake-up call if you anticipate any difficulty rising in time to eat (if you desire), primp, and get to the start.
  • Make certain you know how you're getting to the start and how you're getting home from the finish. At some races, there will be shuttle buses from different locations to the start. This information should be available
  • Plan what you're going to wear, based on current weather conditions. Don't overdress: You don't want to be overheated later in the race. To keep yourself comfortable while you're waiting around for the start, bring an old long-sleeve T-shirt or sweatshirt that you can discard after the race starts (or leave it on if it's too cold).
  • Pin your number to your race shirt the night before. That will give you one less thing to do on race morning.
  • Pack a bag that will be waiting for you at the baggage pick-up area at the finish, or with whomever is going to meet you. Make sure you include a bottle of water, some spare change, and warm clothes.
  • Don't vary your diet during race weekend. There's no need to risk having an upset stomach right before the race. Stick with foods that you know work for you.
  • Clip your toenails.
  • Drink plenty of fluids.
  • Don't worry about the weather. Just try to prepare for it. If it's going to rain, bring a hat; if it's cold, wear gloves.
  • Relax as best you can. Practice visualizing yourself during the race, feeling strong and effortless as you conquer the course. Read a good book, watch TV. The less tense you are going into the race, the better you'll run. Don't fret over the physical part. You've got money in the bank. Now it's time to cash the check.

The Marathon Training Plan in Action

I use the same training plan with everyone I coach, from first-time racers to experienced veterans. I believe in increasing aerobic capacity via a steady dose of consistent weekly mileage. Eventually I incorporate lactate threshold work and, for more advanced runners, VO2max workouts. I work with people via fax, telephone, or e-mail. Following is the account of an advanced runner who was able to improve using my plan. Most important, he was able to learn how to train and reach a peak.

Joe Pendleton Plan

Twenty-eight-year-old Joe Pendleton sought advice on how to improve his marathon PR. He called me during November of 1994, just before running the California International Marathon, where he PR'd with a 2:47. His goal was to run under 2:30 at the same race the next year. As with all runners I advise, I asked Joe for samples of his past training, present fitness, and future goals. Looking at his past training logs, I noticed that Joe seemed to break down on a regular basis. His training log indicated that Joe ran too hard on his easy days. He could handle high mileage; it was a matter of finding the proper mix of lactate threshold and VO2max-enhancing workouts that would be the key.

After a month of advancing Joe's aerobic capacity with a steady dose of relaxed mileage, I began to incorporate LT workouts once a week. Joe was about a 35-minute 10K runner at this time, so I wanted him to do his 4- to 5-mile runs and long intervals about 10 seconds slower than his 10K pace, or 5:50 per mile. The rest of his weekly miles were to be run no faster than 6:40 pace, preferably more in the 7-minute range.

After two months of this, along with an occasional road race, Joe set his sights on the marathon. I wanted Joe to build up to doing 10 to 15 percent of his weekly miles at LT pace, which by June of 1995 had dropped to 5:30 per mile. I also wanted to give Joe a taste of VO2max training, so I introduced biweekly workouts, which involved running 2.5 miles of intervals at 5K pace, which was about 5:10. Intervals ranged from 400 to 1,200 meters with a 400-meter recovery jog between each.

In September, I introduced LT training aimed at a sub-2:30 marathon. Table 5 depicts Joe's training in August, four months prior to the race. This core week was followed consistently, with minor variations as raceday drew near.

By the second week of September, the "hard" day was 4 × 1 mile at 5:25 with a 4-minute rest. The first week in September had a hard day of five miles at 5:45 pace. We continued to alternate tempo runs and long repeats, while gradually dropping the pace each week. By late September, I reintroduced VO2max work every other week. Along with a 5-mile tempo run at 5:35 pace, Joe ran 4 × 1,000 in 3:12 three days later. The long run that week was a 17-miler. The next week it would be 20. By October, Joe could handle 3 × 1 mile at 5:20 per mile with a 3-minute rest, or 8 × 800 at 2:30 with a 3-minute rest, followed by 4 × 1,000 in 3:12 three days later. Again, the remainder of the weeks consisted of easy 6 to 8 milers and a long run.

By November, Joe brought his 10K PR down to 33:20 on a difficult course. We were on track for sub-2:30. He started peaking nicely, running 5 × 1 mile at 5:15 pace with a 4-minute rest, and 3 × 2-mile at 10:20 with a 5-minute rest. His weekly mileage was steady and consistently around 80 each week from mid-September until late November.

The marathon was on December 9, so Joe rested for two weeks by decreasing his mileage to about 50 miles a week. He ran a 15-miler two weeks prior and one final tempo run 10 days prior to the race. We decided Joe's best bet to break 2:30 would be running evenly the whole way. I advised that a bit of conservative early running would be prudent because of the downhill nature of the first half.

Joe went out at 5:45 (2:30:45) pace and had the great fortune of a perfect day and a group of speedy Olympic Trials qualifying-time seekers who would become "dead meat" during the latter stages of the race. He worked himself into 31st by the finish and ran 2:29:40, a whopping 18-minute PR.

Says Joe, "The workouts have kept me from injuring myself as I've done in the past. All of the components in the training plan were brought in slowly and progressively. More importantly, I was able to set a mental foundation for future improvement."

Table 5 — Week of Joe Pendleton's Core Training

Day Workout Purpose Pace
1 8 miles Recovery, RE 6:40 to 7:00 per mile
2 8 miles Recovery, RE 6:40 to 7:00 per mile
3 2 miles, then 4 × 1 mile, then 2 miles Lactate threshold 5:30 per mile, with 3-minutes rest between miles
4 8 miles Recovery, RE 6:40 to 7:00 per mile
5 7 miles Recovery, RE 6:40 to 7:00 per mile
6 7 miles Recovery, RE 6:40 to 7:00 per mile
7 20 miles Strength for marathon 6:40 to 7:00 per mile

figures.caption: Figure 1. Training variables and marathon outcomes: 1. Aerobic capacity training (running economy), 2. Lactate threshold training, and 3. V̇O₂max training lead to Marathon, which leads to 4. Successful completion, 5. Personal best, and 6. Awards, honors, money.

articles.title: Cobras and Concrete
articles.body_html:
"YOU DIE!!!!" I heard the spectators screaming at me with apparent glee as I staggered through the final sweltering mile of the Taipei Marathon. That they were correct in their assessment was indisputable. I had been dead, in fact, for several miles and only wished it were literally true.

Still, even in my broken mental state, I thought it cruel of them to so berate my bleeding corpse. There was so much about this culture I didn't understand (and hadn't understood) since setting foot on this (truly) godforsaken island.

Several months earlier, the Chinese Track and Field Association had invited me to their centerpiece event. I had always wanted to travel to the Orient, and with a reasonable shot at top-10 prize money, plus a sugar daddy sponsor fronting airfare for two and numerous perks, I jumped at the chance.

Not that I was blind to the hazards of running a marathon in a foreign land. I had recently competed in England, where the night-before-the-race meal was roast beef instead of pasta; this, at least, was better than the postrace fare of lemon wedges in Milan. Still, Taipei, Taiwan, presented new realms of experience for this Western man.

For starters, take the plane ride—if you dare. Just as a novice can't understand that marathon racing doesn't begin until mile 20, those who haven't flown to the other side of the globe can't appreciate this particular horror until they experience it firsthand. At least I wasn't in my seat the entire trip. I spent the last hour in the lavatory yaking my guts out while flight attendants pounded on the door in a vain effort to extract me.

My stomach touched down a short while after the plane, and I emerged from my stainless steel stronghold feeling like a cross between John Glenn—man in a can—and Christopher Columbus, not knowing whether the greatest danger lay behind or just ahead. Nevertheless, I was pleased to be on terra firma in Taipei.

With my internal clock turned on its head, and not knowing whether it was night or day, I thought a short run would help orient me, so to speak. Besides, I had a nine-year consecutive-day streak to maintain.

A SERPENTINE RUN

After peeling off my travelling clothes and tossing them into the "burn" pile, I laced up my Brooks, and headed out of the hotel. I carefully picked a path through the chaotic tangle of streets, cars, and unfettered mercantilism that is downtown Taipei. Within moments, I was hopelessly lost.

Taipei is not runner-friendly. Traffic included cars, buses, mopeds, mobile food carts, drunks, mangy dogs, and even mangier prostitutes. After 20 minutes of bouncing around like a pinball, I found myself in a bazaar (and bizarre) area I recognized from guidebook descriptions as Snake Alley, one of the "must see" attractions.

A large crowd had gathered at a brightly lit, midway-style booth. I could read signs in English admonishing, "No Photos." My interest thus piqued, I considered myself fortunate to have stumbled upon an authentic Taiwanese cultural attraction so early in my trip.

Ignoring the stares precipitated by my running attire, round eyes, and Brobdingnagian stature (I'm 5' 10"), I easily peered over the crowd and spied a snake charmer working his magic on a cobra. Based on his incantations and gesticulations, I could tell that the snake hustler was working up to something big.

Suddenly, he grasped the snake with both hands and twisted the reptile's neck, killing it instantly. He then suspended the lifeless body from a wire with a large clip.

To the steady accompaniment of his Chinese patter, the snake monger next brandished a knife. With the precision of a surgeon, he slit open the serpent and poured a surprising amount of blood into a large pitcher. The little man then squeezed the limp snake to find and remove the internal organs, which were added to the scarlet concoction. Finally, like some demented sommelier, he filled half a dozen shot glasses for the enthralled onlookers.

As my Mandarin was more than a little wanting, the point of this unsavory spectacle was lost on me. My green pallor notwithstanding, Snake Man flashed his steel teeth at me and proffered a glass. This particular onlooker, not feeling especially eager for culinary adventures, politely declined. On quaking legs, I jogged back toward my hotel with my second queasy stomach in as many hours.

ORIENTAL CAPITALISM

Taipei, a city of more than three million people, has all the public amenities of one of the moons of Jupiter. While the Taiwanese have embraced the philosophical principles of capitalism, economic growth there is unleavened by Western social norms and niceties. Practically speaking, running downtown sucked.

My gracious hosts had anticipated the hazards of city running and arranged transportation to workout sessions at the city university. Unfortunately, the bus left at 5:30 A.M. for a track an hour away. Ever sensible, I slept in and skipped the organized workouts.

Instead, I risked a short run to a bridge over a brackish river visible from my hotel window. Upon closer inspection, I thought the seething effluent could have been a tributary of the River Styx. Still, I was on the only unobstructed, runable stretch of road in the city, to which the sulfuric stench and roar of moped traffic were only minor irritants.

Ignoring abusive remarks and gestures from the drivers was easy, too—that was just like home. My fear was that some motorist would knock me into the festering waters. Nonetheless, I was grateful to have an uninterrupted running route. Like some demented bridge master, I trekked back and forth for 30 minutes every day.

VENUE INSPECTION

The day before the race, the invited runners were taken on a "venue inspection." Several vans took us past dozens of landfills—urban planning is apparently an unpracticed discipline here—to the outskirts of Taipei. Another few miles of off-road maneuvering brought us to the race site.

[[FIGURE:1]]

Asians are renowned for their linear thinking, and the course layout reflected that inclination. Tollbooths marked the starting line, and the route then traced a straight shot out the turnpike. An orange traffic cone served as the turnaround point 13.1 miles later, after which we were to retrace our steps to the start.

We tried to maintain our game faces, and we joked about running with tokens or a roll of quarters. But many of the runners weren't laughing, and others swore quietly. As we surveyed the starting area, intense winds sent giant multicolored tents skittering across the toll plaza and toward the vans. The drivers quickly restarted the vehicles and careened away from the runaway tents just before the tents slammed over a guardrail and crumpled into a ditch beside the road. My hopes for a fast, honest marathon were similarly crushed.

Although we had all seen too much already, the organizers insisted on driving the entire course. Actually, it turned out to be not unlike the New Jersey Turnpike, but eerily devoid of traffic: three lanes in each direction, wide shoulders, exit ramps, and a couple of toll plazas. Imagine a marathon on Interstate 95 from Seacaucus to Hoboken and back.

Although a hard surface—concrete, no less—was in place for the entire course, in many spots the highway was still under construction, with loose

gravel, dirt, silt, and standing water. Construction workers in conical straw hats gaped at the procession of vans plowing resolutely through the mess.

At the halfway mark, we saw a large sign fashioned from bamboo poles that had been laid flat by the wind. Inside the vans, the mood shifted from anticipation and nervous good humor to disbelief and brooding. The rest of the venue inspection did little to alleviate our initial misgivings.

THE NIGHT BEFORE THE DAY AFTER

Back at the hotel that evening, gallows humor dominated dinner conversation. We joked about trading racing flats for racing slicks, Goodyear sponsorship, and land sailing. You're absolutely correct: It wasn't funny.

One more thing: Taiwan is typically very hot and humid. With the race scheduled to start at 6:30 A.M. to beat the heat, we went to bed early, contemplating our sins, fearing a just God, and dreaming of home.

At 2:15 the next morning, the telephone startled me to semiconsciousness. It was a race official calling to say that breakfast was being served. Never mind that I didn't want to eat or that I had set my own alarm for the slightly more reasonable hour of 4:00 A.M.—I got up.

With the moon still high, the vans took us back to the race site, which looked even more desolate in the darkness. Two huge construction cranes had been deployed to create an ominous postindustrial finishing chute, which looked to be the East's answer to the West's frivolous balloon arches.

All week we had asked how many runners were entered in the race. Official estimates ranged from 2,000 to 10,000. Apparently raceday entries were less than expected—only 400 starters assembled at the toll plaza. Not surprisingly, given the course location and layout, spectators were few and far between.

As the sun and temperatures began to rise just before the start, I drank as much as I could hold, doused myself with water, and hoped for the best. The Europeans and Canadians continued to grouse, right up until the start. We assumed the African and Eastern European runners were equally discontent, but they seemed to be keeping it to themselves.

A police whistle set us on our way. The first 10 miles were predictably uneventful, if not out-and-out monotonous. At least nobody got lost. For a small island country, however, Taiwan sure seemed to have a lot of hills.

As we circled the cone at the halfway point, all competitors were given a blue wristband to indicate that we'd indeed run that far. This safeguard seemed unnecessary, given the chances of anyone finding the course, much less sneaking onto it.

The handful of spectators made up in enthusiasm what they lacked in numbers. Several clapped and shouted, and I felt a kinship with those citizens foolish enough to journey out onto the course. Some held handmade signs with Chinese characters. I imagined them to say, "Go Dad" and "Everyone's A Winner." A few English-lettered signs also marked the course. Although it probably lost something in the translation, my favorite was "Go Able Men."

CHANGE OF STATUS

Despite 70-degree temperatures, infrequent aid stations, and meaningless splits (17.5, 22.5, and 27.5 kilometers), I felt reasonably strong. By halfway, I was in seventh place and ready to move up. Around 18 miles, however, a pack of five came out of nowhere to join me and the Belgian with whom I'd been maintaining a steady and honest pace. The panic that I had struggled to keep in check since before the plane even touched down was erupting again.

I checked out my competition—of course, they looked great. The foreigners did, too. Never mind that I was a stranger in this strange land. Two miles later, my cockpit lights started flashing, and my veneer of optimism crumpled. In short order, I found myself out of the money, struggling to maintain contact, and finally shooting out the back door.

My formerly fond feelings toward the spectators soured, and their indecipherable signs seemed suddenly mocking. By the 23rd mile, their cries of "Yeedie!" rang in my frayed brain as "You die!!!" I shuffled home at almost six minutes per mile, finishing in 14th place with a 2:22.

Four Africans, a Korean, and a Pole took six of the first eight places. The Western runners, although evenly matched in numbers and on paper, could not produce under the difficult mental and physical conditions. Andrej Witczak's

[[FIGURE:1]]

$7,000 third place money would pay rent and buy groceries for years in Poland; not one of the North Americans or Western Europeans had incentive like that.

That's not to make excuses for my sorry performance, but simply to illustrate what Western runners seemed to lack all week: an all-consuming drive to succeed. In the face of adverse conditions, the Western runners faded like cheap Taiwanese suits.

The urgency of the African and Eastern European runners became evident at crunch time on the Taipei highway. The real competitors tapped into their heritage and cultural mettle to fight on, while this runner said "No thanks" and cashed in his chips. But all may not be lost, so long as I recognized, absorbed, and can someday invoke even a bit of their determination.

figures.caption: Hage joins the other elite runners for a venue inspection the day before the race.
Hage shuffles home, finishing in 14th place with a 2:22.

articles.title: The Last PR
articles.body_html:
WOULDN'T LIFE be wonderful if we could expect a never-ending string of personal records (PRs) throughout our running careers? We might not want a new PR every time we raced, of course—that would soon become boring and would devalue the effort involved in training for better races. But wouldn't it be nice to be able to set new PRs in our 40s, 50s, 60s, or however long we continue to race?

Unfortunately, as we all know, becoming faster forever is a concept comparable to the fabled Perpetual Motion Machine. The reality is more like this scenario: First, we enjoy a string of PRs in our early running years, as we condition our bodies and hone our running skills. Then, we reach and maintain a plateau, during which time we might occasionally see a new PR on an exceptional day when everything clicks. Finally, we go through the "long downhill slide," with no PRs and an increasing frequency of PW (personal worst) performances.

There are many reasons PRs tend to disappear. A common reason is injury—usually an overuse injury due to improper training techniques, excessive mileage, or participation in too many races. At the other end of the spectrum, too little training, often due to constraints on our time because of job and family commitments, can have a negative effect on PR production as well. Finally, and probably the most important factor, is the dreaded, unstoppable aging process.

Even if we stay injury free and find the time for adequate training, we have little control over the aging process. Granted, we can watch our diets carefully and maintain the other elements of a healthy lifestyle, but there are many things about the aging process that we absolutely cannot alter.

For example, if you are now 35 years old, have 20/20 vision, and find it amusing that all of your 45- to 50-year-old friends have reading glasses or bifocals, don't become too complacent. You might think that you will never lose your near vision, but I've got some news for you: Your time will come!

One of my running friends is an ophthalmologist. Several years ago he confided in me that deep inside he was convinced that he would be the "one person in a thousand" who would never need reading glasses, even though his medical training told him otherwise. You can guess the rest of the story. He managed to make it to age 45 without glasses, but then he faced the choice between never reading anything again or admitting defeat to the "god of presbyopia."

One can only wonder how many other things that happen to the aging body—some known to science and some yet undiscovered—profoundly affect our running ability. Even something as innocuous as the aforementioned presbyopia can cause problems during races, especially long trail races that have cutoff times. Aging runners with normal distance vision will find that they cannot read their watches and, therefore, cannot tell if they are dangerously close to missing a cutoff time. If they wear reading glasses so they can see their watches, then the trail is blurry, and they risk tripping on rocks or tree roots.

Nearsighted aging runners in the same situation have a similar choice: Wear bifocals so they can see their watches, and then put up with a blurry view of the trail, or wear single-vision glasses, which provide a clear view of the trail but a blurry view of their watches. Actually, in this situation the nearsighted runner may have an advantage because many bifocal wearers can wear their single-vision glasses for distance and still see close up by removing their glasses.

So if you see older runners out on the trail in the middle of the night with their glasses on their foreheads and their flashlights shining toward their watches, you will know what is happening. Being able to read watches at a glance is something younger runners take for granted.

THE MARCH OF YEARS

The aging process causes other problems for runners, most of them far more serious than not being able to read a stopwatch. For example, the ability to heal quickly and completely diminishes as the body ages. Runners over the age of 40 who use the same rigorous training regimen that they used in their younger years are playing a dangerous game of "injury roulette." All it takes is the wrong combination of training too hard, racing too often, and allowing an inadequate number of recovery days, and BANG!!, you've sustained an injury that can take seemingly forever to heal. If you are really unlucky, the injury may end your running career.

I have a fair number of friends with whom I've enjoyed many a spirited race in years past; now many are no longer able to run because of injuries. Most of them have switched to bicycling, which is also a fine sport; but when we talk, it is easy to tell that they miss running those 10Ks, marathons, and ultras.

And then there is the gradual loss in aerobic capacity and muscle strength that accompanies aging. Fortunately, some scientific studies have shown that these losses progress more slowly in people who exercise regularly. This is good news for runners, especially those who also weight train. The bad news is that strength and aerobic capacity still diminish to some extent with age, even for people who are active and fit. This does not bode well for people who are trying to set PRs.

The evidence that runners become slower as they age is virtually indisputable. There may be some exceptions to this rule—for example, people who don't start running until their 40s or 50s and who are, therefore, still in the PR-setting phase of their careers. But the truth is that they, too, will eventually slow because of age. As runners, we must eventually face the physical and psychological realities of aging and their effect on our running performance. This leads us to a big question: How can we best deal with the fact that we may have seen our last PR?

Fortunately, there are many ways we can deal with the toll that aging takes on our inherent running speed. I lump these strategies into two categories—physical and psychological.

PHYSICAL STRATEGIES

Avoid Injuries

Staying injury-free is critical for the aging runner. One way to avoid injury is to eliminate running on consecutive days. Either use the "off" days for aerobic cross-training (such as bicycling or cross-country skiing) or for weight workouts. Besides providing a break from the pounding that your legs take during running, weight training will strengthen your muscles, which can provide additional protection from muscle and joint injuries.

Another way to avoid injury, as mentioned earlier, is to use common sense regarding the amount of training mileage, the intensity of training, and the intensity and frequency of races. Running every other day automatically takes care of the training mileage issue, as long as you don't try to double your mileage on the "on" days to make up for the off days. The same goes for the intensity of the workout. You might be able to increase the intensity somewhat if you run only every other day, but don't increase the intensity drastically, or you will defeat the purpose of the rest day. Finally, when selecting your races, allow enough recovery time between events. Racing every weekend is probably not a good idea, unless you run the races truly at training speed, not racing speed.

Eat Properly and Get Sufficient Rest

Not much needs to be said here. As a group, runners are generally very conscientious when it comes to proper nutrition and the need for rest. With our busy lives, getting enough rest is always a challenge, but most of us have found that we run better and avoid illness more effectively if we get enough sleep.

Regarding vitamin supplements, in recent years the medical community has reversed its former position that vitamins are not necessary if we eat a healthy, balanced diet. Scientific evidence now suggests that the vitamin advocates were probably on the right track.

Moderation certainly applies to vitamin intake, however. Taking a multivitamin tablet, a 1,000-milligram tablet of vitamin C, and 400 international units of vitamin E daily seems to have increased my own resistance to illness considerably, compared to the days when I followed the former medical wisdom and avoided vitamin supplements entirely. While we may not see any more PRs, those of us who get proper nutrition and rest are likely to maintain a performance level that is higher than those who neglect these important areas of life.

Run Efficiently and Effectively

Learning how to make the most of the ability you have is something that comes with experience, and experience is one of the advantages that aging runners have. Like aging racquetball players who may be slow and out of shape but who still beat younger, faster opponents through sheer craftiness (i.e., "Hit the ball where they ain't!"), experienced runners who use their heads can enjoy some very satisfying results.

In the shorter 5K and 10K events, the most important factor, aside from proper training, is proper pacing. Most experienced runners know that starting fast and then trying to "hang on" almost never works as well as starting at a moderate pace, increasing the pace gradually, and then finishing with a powerful kick.

In the longer events, such as the marathon, drinking the proper amount and types of fluids comes into play. For some marathoners, food intake during the race is also a factor in racing success. The more experience a runner has, the easier it is to design a strategy that will work.

In ultramarathons, especially in 100-mile trail races, there are a myriad of factors that can provide opportunities for crafty, aging runners to make up enough time to finish well ahead of the stronger but less knowledgeable runners. Some examples include knowing which shoes to wear, what kind of food to eat and when to eat it, where to have your crew meet you, when to be conservative and walk, and when to pull out the stops and go for it.

Pick the Right Distance

The longer the distance of the race, the more opportunities aging runners have to apply hard-earned knowledge, including all the sneaky tricks they have learned over the years. I have observed that, relatively speaking, older runners tend to perform much better in ultramarathons than they do in 5Ks, 10Ks, and marathons. Furthermore, the longer the ultra event, the better they seem to do.

If you are an older runner who has always focused on short-distance races, you may want to consider trying the longer distances. There is a side benefit, too. If you race at a new distance, a PR is a given if you merely finish the event.

I have experienced this phenomenon personally. After being a mediocre short-distance runner for many years, I discovered that I could do relatively better in 50-mile and 100K races. When I switched my emphasis to 100-mile trail races, I had even more success, which I attribute to an ability to pay attention to the many details associated with these events and to a previously-undiscovered gift of mental toughness. Then I tried several 24-hour and 48-hour races, which turned out to be events in which I actually could place in the top five overall and even occasionally win outright.

This success has been difficult to comprehend for someone who in 5K and 10K events rarely placed high enough to earn even a third-place age-group award. But I must admit that I don't mind finishing near the top of the list for a change.

The point is that you will never know what your best race distance is until you've tried them all, and aging runners tend to perform better in the long events where experience and judgment may be more important than aerobic capacity.

PSYCHOLOGICAL STRATEGIES

Look at Running from a Different Perspective

Instead of feeling sorry for ourselves because we can't run a 10K or a marathon as fast as we could at age 20 or 30, we aging runners would do well to count our blessings. Think about all the former runners who can no longer run at all because of sickness or injury. If we realize that just being able to run at all is a blessing, the fact that we can't run as fast as we could when we were younger becomes less important. Try to view all races the way ultrarunners have traditionally looked at ultramarathons: anyone who finishes is a "winner."

Compete in Age Groups, Not in the Open Division

Aging runners who still feel a strong urge to compete can train their minds to ignore the open division and focus instead on age groups. Try to exclude all competitors from your consciousness except those in your own age group.

On the other hand, if you are typically last in your age group, you can compare yourself to other age groups. Check the race results to see how many younger runners of the same or opposite sex finished behind you. And even if you finished dead last in the race, you can compare yourself to the runners who DNFed or to the sedentary folks your age who stayed at home.

You also can use age-adjusted performance charts to see how your current performances compare to those of previous years, after applying the age-adjustment factor. The trick is to find your victories where you can, even if the only victory you can claim is that you finished before the race director pulled the plug on the timing clock.

Set Attainable Goals

One school of thought recommends setting lofty goals in order to pressure yourself into high performance. Personally, I have had more success by taking the pressure off myself and setting goals that are readily attainable. When running a marathon or ultramarathon, I usually carry a split time sheet with five columns. The middle column displays split times for my probable pace for the event, using a conservative estimate of my current ability.

To the right are two columns displaying splits for two slower paces, one for a "bad" day and one for a "terrible" day. To the left of the center column is a column with a faster pace than my anticipated pace, just in case I am having a "good" day. To the far left is a faster "dream" pace, in the event that I find myself having a "fantastic" day.

More often than not, I am able to run at least as fast as my goal pace in the middle column, and once in a while I even surprise myself and maintain the pace in the "dream" column. Were I to carry a split time sheet containing only my "dream" splits, I would usually be far off that pace, which is demoralizing. Any runner who becomes demoralized at mile five in a marathon or a 50-miler is not likely to have a successful race. Expecting less and accomplishing more is much easier and sweeter for the psyche than expecting more and accomplishing less, and few would deny that the condition of the psyche is directly related to performance.

[[FIGURE:1]]

If you are an unfortunate aging runner who has sustained a long-term injury, you can make your goals attainable by keeping two sets of PRs—a pre-injury set and a post-injury set. My mountaineering/backcountry skiing friend Al sustained such an injury, a persistent plantar fasciitis, about 15 years ago. Before the injury he was able to run a 40-minute 10K, but the injury has forced him to give up running almost entirely. Now he occasionally tries a 10K for old times' sake, but his goal has shifted to the 50-minute barrier. This seems reasonable, considering the severity and duration of his injury.

Allow for Miracles

Strange things happen in the lives of runners. After years of slower and slower race times, sometimes we find ourselves enjoying a period during which some of that lost speed miraculously reappears for a time. Either by accident or design, maybe we have hit just the right balance of training, nutrition, mental attitude, and other factors, and suddenly we are running faster than we have for many years.

I experienced something like this recently. Maybe it was the large garden we raised this year, our first really good one in more than 10 years. There was so much produce that I spent the summer and fall eating large quantities of fresh fruits and vegetables, which left room in my diet for only small amounts of meats and other fatty foods.

Unintentionally, I found myself dropping seven or eight pounds, and at the same time I noticed that my training runs were becoming faster and easier. Soon thereafter I ran a half-marathon in heavy rain on a tough, hilly course and came within a minute of the PR I had set in 1984 at the peak of my running prowess. Unfortunately, just as I was about to have a go at my old 10-mile and marathon PRs, my lucky streak ended when I caught a bad cold; but it was great while it lasted.

If you are fortunate enough to experience such a phenomenon, enjoy it; but beyond that, try to determine why it happened and use the information to your advantage. Perhaps your PRs aren't a thing of the past.

CONCLUSIONS

By keeping ourselves physically healthy and by developing a mental attitude that focuses on the positive aspects of our running, aging runners can enjoy training and racing even if we have long since seen our last PR. We should also remember that trying new distances will guarantee a new PR and will provide us with an opportunity to determine which distance is best for us as individuals. Above all, as long as we can still run, we are winners, and every day that we are still running is a new PR for the number of days run in our lifetime!

figures.caption: Knowing when to meet your crew in an ultra event comes with years of experience.

articles.title: Secrets of Marathoning: The Top 13 Ways to Mess Up Your Marathon Debut
articles.body_html:
SWEATING THROUGH 16 or 18 weeks of progressively more difficult workouts as you prepare for your first marathon is easy when compared to the effort you'll need to exercise restraint, self-control, and discipline in the final days before the race. Your baser instincts will tempt you, so you've got to be prepared to show your true mettle during race weekend. Resist the inner voice that tempts you to do the following:

  • 13. LOOK GOOD. Because this is your one and only first-ever marathon, you will want to look especially good for the photographers at the start, along the course, and at the finish line. So wear a brand, spanking new running outfit and new running shoes. No sense using the ratty stuff you've been wearing on your long runs. In fact, how about that new outfit your friends bought for you? You know, the friends who told you that you were crazy when you announced that you were going to train for a marathon. The same friends who, now that you've gotten this far unscathed, wanted to jump on the bandwagon so they bought you a new running outfit that looks like it came from the Frederick's of Hollywood catalog. Look good because those race photos are going to be something you'll treasure for the rest of your life.
  • 12. PAINT THE TOWN. You and your friends purposely picked a destination marathon for your debut, so what's the point of spending Saturday night in your hotel room? Walk the town, see the sights, experience everything. It's all part of your one-and-only first-time marathon experience. Make memories.
  • 11. EAT FASHIONABLY LATE. The fresher the carbohydrates you load the night before your race, the better they'll do their job at 18 miles. Runners who eat really early the night before the race are going to be running on stale carbs.
  • 10. WINELOAD. You've all read the studies touting the benefits of wine, especially red wine. If wine is good for your heart, think of how happy your heart will be during the race when it's soaked in delicious zinfandel. Don't worry about the dehydrating effects of wineloading—the entry form says there are plenty of aid stations along the race course.
  • 9. PARTY HARDY. You'll only have one first marathon, and this is it, so make it memorable by partying down once you're back in your room after your late dinner. Besides, all the literature says that you aren't going to sleep well the night before your race anyway.
  • 8. SLEEP IN. Try to cut the time between when you crawl out of bed and when you line up at the starting line as close as possible. This strategy has two important benefits: It delivers you fresh to the starting line, and it brings your heart up to speed as you rush around to get there under the wire.
  • 7. SUGARLOAD. Simple sugar is premium fuel for the marathoner late in the race. But why wait until then? Before the race, eat a donut or two, dump a few packets of sugar into your coffee—get that insulin reaction going early. The rest of your body is up and ready to run, so there's no reason to let the pancreas lay back and relax.
  • 6. STRETCH 'TIL IT HURTS. Before the start, stretch until it hurts. No pain, no gain. Cold muscles need to be stretched so they're flexible enough to get you out of the blocks quickly. Ballistic stretching gets the job done faster. Ignore the sounds of fraying connective tissue—it's just your body's way of saying, "Hey! I'm ready!"
  • 5. DO 10 × 200 METERS FAST. Stretching alone can't get you ready. You need to get some blood pumping. Find a quiet place a quarter-mile or so from the start and knock off some good 200-meter sprints. This secret exercise will help you get off the starting line more efficiently.
  • 4. LINE UP IN FRONT. What's the profit of struggling to make your way through hundreds of bodies toward the front after the race begins when you can start in the front and let the other runners do all the work trying to get past you? Besides, lining up in front puts you that much closer to the finish line, and photographers like to shoot the start of the race. They don't shoot pictures of people who are back in the crowd.
  • 3. GO WITH YOUR ADRENALINE. Dr. George Sheehan always preached that you should "Listen to your body." That's bunk. Your body says, "Go!" Your adrenaline is high. You're high. Your body knows best, so go with how you feel. And you feel GREAT! Besides, a one-mile PR for the first mile of a marathon is a real upper!
  • 2. SPRINT DRAMATICALLY THROUGH AID STATIONS. Some of the most dramatic marathon photos show runners rushing through an aid station, water or Gatorade caught in midwave as it crests over the lip of the cup. Sprinting through aid stations also lets you pass people while they waste their time stopping to get fluids.
  • 1. PUT MINUTES IN THE BANK. The sweet spot in a marathon comes between miles 7 and 15, when everything is warmed up and you are running smoothly and strong. This is really the time to run like you feel. Pick it up. Put those extra minutes in the bank as a reserve for when you'll naturally slow down at the end of the race.

Bonus Secret: Don't bother to carry money in the pouch of your running shorts in case of an emergency. When you drop out at 19 miles, your pitiful, limping, exhausted form spiffed out in those now bedraggled new running clothes will elicit sympathy in some motorist's heart; and you'll be driven, kindly and slowly, to the finish line that you nearly reached under your own power. Go for it!

articles.title: The Great Six-Day Races
articles.body_html:
Here's a quick summary of Part I of The Great Six-Day Races, which appeared in the premier issue of Marathon & Beyond. — Editor

In the late 18th and early 19th centuries, eccentric athletes known as "pedestrians" extended the concept of how far man could travel under his own power. Englishman Foster Powell covered 100 miles in 22 hours in 1788, and fellow Brit Captain Barclay surpassed that by covering 100 miles in 19 hours in 1806. Those marks were made to seem . . . well . . . almost pedestrian in 1874 when Edward Payson Weston walked 500 miles in six days at the Washington Street Rink in Newark, New Jersey. Fired by Weston's accomplishments, Chicago Irish immigrant Daniel O'Leary set out to overtake Weston. In November 1875 the two met in a six-day duel, which O'Leary won by covering 503.33 miles to Weston's 451.6 miles. Weston, who had never wanted to be tagged "champion" in the first place, had been vanquished. Both men looked east, to England, to find new worlds to conquer.

CHAPTER 3: TWO AMERICANS STORM BRITAIN

The English sporting press refused to believe the accomplishments of Daniel O'Leary and Edward Payson Weston. In fact, many were willing to wager large sums of money that neither O'Leary nor Weston could duplicate their performances when watched by English scorers.

Weston, aware that he was now a dethroned king and considered by many in the United States as an over-the-hill failure at age 36, sailed for England in January of 1876. While Weston had been soundly defeated by O'Leary, he knew that there were, at present, no English walkers or even runners who could match him over an extended trial.

Weston arrived in England possessing an astounding but suspect reputation. The British sporting elite were absolutely convinced that no man, especially an American, could fairly cover 500 miles in six days. Their contempt for the athletic prowess of Americans shouts from the pages of Lancet, a well-respected British medical journal:

But with characteristic go-a-headness it has been reserved for the Americans to develop conditions far beyond other nations in their hygienic unwholesomeness. Living habitually in their close, stove-heated rooms, bolting their food at railway speed. . . year by year Americans grow thinner, lighter, and shorter lived. Rowing and other athletics, with the exception of skating and baseball, are both despised and neglected in America.

Weston's first match in England took place in Agricultural Hall, Islington, London, on February 8, 1876. It was a 24-hour competition against William Perkins, "the Champion of England." Weston won with 109 miles, walking Perkins to a standstill. Then, with only five days of rest, Weston walked 180.5 miles in 48 hours on February 15 and 16. At the conclusion of the event, there was "no evidence of distress perceptible. He [Weston] appeared active, cheerful, and made an appropriately expressed speech in which his tongue moved as nimbly as his legs had previously done."

Again Weston took only five days of rest before his next competition—a walk of 275 miles within 72 hours against a little-known Englishman, Charles Rowell. During a rest period on the second day, Weston read some letters and transacted some urgent business. In the evening he led the band around the hall himself, accompanying them on the cornet. This was to demonstrate that he still possessed a great reserve of strength and was not afraid to call upon it. Within three minutes and 39 seconds of the allotted time, Weston went on to complete

[[FIGURE:1]]

275 miles. Rowell covered a mere 176 miles. Weston had walked the first 90 and the last 60 miles without a rest.

This walk, though, was not as easy as the previous two. The effort left Weston quite exhausted. He came down with a bad cold and fever for two days. He was back, however, circling the seven-lap, gravel-and-loam track inside Agricultural Hall just 10 days later. He was going to attempt to duplicate his Newark feat of walking 500 miles in six days.

The British countered with three of their best pedestrians: Newman, Martin, and Taylor. Weston again triumphed, but he walked only 450 miles. Extenuating circumstances affected this last effort by Weston, as the Lancet (now enamored with this American) tells us:

Mr. Weston has performed, not the feat which he proposed, but another which, we think, more remarkable. He has walked with a sprained, painful, and considerably distended knee-joint in the middle of the period. The praise is more justified, as Mr. Weston was from the outset very insufficiently recovered from a feverish cold which confined him to his house at Brighton with a 100-degree temperature. . . At eleven o'clock on Saturday night the pace was that of a man with whom few of us would like to walk far.

Weston had now covered 1,015 miles in competition in five consecutive weeks before 200,000 people. Weston was under constant scrutiny by the British medical profession during each of these four contests. Everything that he ingested and excreted was measured and analyzed. Numerous articles were written in Lancet, The British Medical Journal, and even the Journal of the Royal Society of England. Dr. F.W. Pavy summarized the feelings of the medical men: "We have great pleasure in bearing testimony to the genial and cheerful spirit of Mr. Weston, to the entire absence of coarseness in his athleticism, and his willingness to make his walking feats subservient to scientific purposes." Weston had earned credibility among the British sporting elite.

Weston walked 450 miles in six days once more in Edinburgh, Scotland, from June 19 to June 25, 1876. He then accomplished what all Great Britain had been waiting for. From September 25 to 30 at the Toxteth Park, in Liverpool, he covered 501 miles within 144 hours.

In the eight months that Weston had been in England he had won $30,000 and made a host of rich, influential friends. One of these was Parliament member Sir John Dugdale Astley, who was known as the "Sporting Baron." Astley was the epitome of the Victorian sporting gentleman. He had a large stable of horses, which he raced often, and he financially supported any athletic endeavor he considered worthy.
